source_metadata.title_raw: Dwight L. Moore - I-19-044 - March 27, 2019 - Paradise

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Dwight L. Moore 
Town Attorney 
5555 Skyway 
Paradise, CA 95969-4931 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. 1-19-044 
Dear Mr. Moore: 
March 27, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Councilmember Steve Crowder, 
regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Because your 
question is general in nature, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance. 2 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
May Town Councilmember Crowder take part in the selection, formation, or governance of 
a proposed sewer/assessment district that would serve specific commercial real property areas 
within your jurisdiction, given that he owns commercial real property within the proposed 
sewer/assessment district? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. Given that the proposed sewer/assessment district would cover 90 percent of 
commercial real property within the jurisdiction, Councilmember Crowder may take part in 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation l 8329(c)(3).) 


File No. I-19-044 
Page No. 2 
governmental decisions pertaining to the selection, formation, and governance of the district to the 
extent that his property and business will not be uniquely affected in comparison to other properties 
and businesses subject to the assessment. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
The Town of Paradise is governed by a Town Council with five elected members. Currently, 
the Town does not provide any centralized wastewater treatment services to real properties within 
the Town limits. Improved real properties are served by private septic systems. The Town is 
currently conducting a comprehensive sewer wastewater study pertaining to various options relating 
to the treatment of wastewater within proposed commercial areas. 
Implementation of the selected option may require the Town Council to form a 
sewer/assessment district and Town wastewater public utility service for properties within the 
district. In your initial request for advice, you mentioned that the various study areas include more 
than 90 percent of the jurisdiction's commercial real property. In a follow-up phone call, you 
clarified that the proposed sewer/assessment district is the same as these study areas (referred to on 
your attached map as "proposed service area") and, thus, would include 90 percent of the 
jurisdiction's commercial real property. 
To finance the construction of any sewer infrastructure related to the district, it may be 
necessary to issue bonds. There would also most likely be sewer connection and operating fees that 
would need to be adopted by the Town Council. 
Councilmember Steve Crowder currently owns one piece of commercial real property 
within the proposed sewer/assessment district. His interest in the property is worth more than 
$2,000. The parcel of land previously had a commercial building on it, but that building was 
destroyed by the "Camp Fire" on November 8, 2018. 
ANALYSIS 
Under Section 87100, "[n]o public official at any level of state or local government shall 
make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a 
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest." 
Interests 
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if 
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable 
from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or 
on an enumerated interest. (Section 87103.) Such interests include: 
• 
Any real property in which the public official has an interest worth $2,000 or more. 
(Section 87103(a).) 
• 
Any business entity in which the official has an investment worth $2,000 or more or is a 
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or manager. (Section 87103(b) and (d).) 


File No. I-19-044 
Page No. 3 
• 
Any source of income aggregating to $500 or more in value provided or promised to, 
received by, the public official within 13 months prior to the time when the decision is 
made. (Section 87103(c).) 
Here, Councilmember Crowder has an economic interest in his real property, which is worth 
more than $2,000. As a commercial property, it also appears that Councilmember Crowder has 
economic interests in a business as both a business entity and source of income, as well as potential 
interest in sources of income to his business entity. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is 
"explicitly involved" in a decision. An interest is "explicitly involved" in a decision if the interest is 
a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's 
agency. (Regulation 1870l(a).) Real property is the subject of a decision when "the decision 
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other 
entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest, and includes any governmental decision 
affecting a real property financial interest as described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l)-(6). 
If the interest is "not explicitly involved" in the decision, a financial effect is reasonably 
foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical. 
Regulation 18702.23 provides that the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on a real property in which an official has a financial interest is material 
under certain enumerated circumstances. The regulation provides that the effect is material 
whenever the governmental decision: 
• 
Determines the parcel's zoning or rezoning, other than a zoning decision applicable to all 
properties designated in that category; annexation or de-annexation; inclusion or exclusion 
from any city, county, district, or local government subdivision or other boundaries, other 
than elective district boundaries (Regulation 18702.2(a)(2)); 
• 
Would impose, repeal, or modify any taxes, fees, or assessments that apply to the parcel 
(Regulation 18702.2(a)(3); or 
• 
Involves construction of, or improvements to, streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or 
similar facilities, and the parcel will receive new or improved services that provide a benefit 
or detriment disproportionate to other properties receiving the services (Regulation 
18702.2(a)(6)). 
Although the creation of a sewer/assessment district would presumably involve the 
construction of, or improvements to, streets, water, and the sewer system within the jurisdiction, 
none of the facts indicate that Councilmember Crowder's property would receive a benefit or 
detriment disproportionate to other properties receiving the services. However, the governmental 
3 Regulation 18702.2 as amended by the Commission on January 17, 2019. 


File No. I-19-044 
Page No. 4 
decisions would determine the boundarie.s of the sewer/assessment district and whether 
Councilmember Crowder's property is included within those boundaries. Likewise, the creation of a 
sewer/assessment district and accompanying public utility service would include the imposition of 
various taxes, fees, and assessments that would apply to Councilmember Crowder's property. 
Accordingly, Councilmember Crowder's property interest is explicitly involved in the decisions and 
any financial effect on the interest is reasonably foreseeable under Regulation 18701(a) and 
material under Regulation 18702.2(a)(2) and (3).)4 
The "Public Generally" Exception 
Even if a public official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the 
governmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is 
indistinguishable from the manner in which it will affect the public generally. (Section 87103; 
Regulation 18703.) Under Regulation 18703, a governmental decision's financial effect on a public 
official's economic interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official 
establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on his or her financial 
interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant segment. (Regulation 18703(a).) A 
significant segment of the public includes at least 25 percent of all real property, commercial real 
property, or residential real property within the official's jurisdiction. (Regulation 18703(b).) 
You have indicated that the Town Council will be faced with the governmental decision of 
whether to implement a sewer/assessment district in order to provide centralized wastewater 
treatment services to specific commercial real property areas within the Town. Councilmember 
Crowder's commercial real property would be included within that district. However, it appears that 
the decisions in question would have the same financial effect on 90 percent of the commercial real 
property in the jurisdiction. Accordingly, to the extent that Councilmember Crowder's interest 
including his property, business, and customers are not impacted differently than other properties 
subject to the proposed assessment or businesses operated from the properties, he may take part in 
governmental decisions pertaining to the selection, formation, and governance of the district under 
the public generally exception. 
Be aware, however, that the "public generally" exception is applied on a decision-by-
decision basis. If the assessment will affect Councilmember's Crowder's property, business, or 
customers in a manner different than the other properties or businesses subject to the assessment, he 
should seek additional advice identifying the specific decision. 
4 In light of our conclusion that Councilmember Crowder is potentially disqualified based upon his interest in 
his property but permitted to participate in the decisions under the public generally exception, it is unnecessary to 
further consider his interests in his business or sources of income to his business. 


File No. I-19-044 
Page No. 5 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
KMC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Kevin Cornwall 
Counsel, Legal Division

governmental decisions pertaining to the selection, formation, and governance of the district to the 
extent that his property and business will not be uniquely affected in comparison to other properties 
and businesses subject to the assessment.

detriment disproportionate to other properties receiving the services. However, the governmental 
3 Regulation 18702.2 as amended by the Commission on January 17, 2019.  
decisions would determine the boundarie.s of the sewer/assessment district and whether 
Councilmember Crowder's property is included within those boundaries. Likewise, the creation of a
